explanation.text: The evening is so hot that no one notices the back door has been left ajar, and its stubborn catch was not pulled shut. Because the door remains open, the bat is able to enter the house.
Everyone is talking, worrying, crying, or whimpering at once while Andrew wants to reassure them. He “couldn’t get a word in edgewise” because the commotion gives him no opportunity to speak.
Andrew asks the adults to wait and hear his idea, and they loosen their grips on the weapons. Mr. Overton even expresses relief, which shows that the characters have become calmer.
Because Andrew knows that bats are nocturnal, darkness inside will feel familiar rather than frightening. He leaves a bright light outside to attract insects and guide the bat toward the open door.
Andrew “turned on the floodlight” beside the open back door. Because a floodlight makes an area bright, that phrase explains that the later “illuminated doorway” is a lighted doorway.

Andrew prevents the adults from killing the bat and creates conditions that lead it safely through the open door. Therefore, the bat’s escape because of Andrew captures the story’s main problem and resolution.

The article says off-road riding requires a bike that can handle rough treatment, then advises asking experienced friends and bike stores what works best. These questions help because riders need to choose a suitable bike.

Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain how to select a sturdy bike, strengthen the needed muscles, warm up, cool down, and stretch. These details support the main idea because they describe preparation for mountain biking.
The sentence lists broadslides, bunny-hops, and jumps as techniques used to overcome trail obstacles. Calling these techniques an “arsenal” therefore means a collection of useful biking skills.
During Trials, judges observe riders and penalize them for putting a foot down, stopping, or falling. The event must be watched closely because officials need to notice and record those mistakes.

Training prepares a rider’s body, equipment knowledge helps the rider choose and repair a bike, and protective gear reduces danger. Together these steps support safely enjoying the sport because they address both skill and safety.

The article repeatedly explains the special equipment, techniques, repairs, competitions, and safety measures needed off-road. Therefore, the statement that trail riding requires skills beyond neighborhood biking best summarizes its main idea.

Paragraph 1 begins by asking readers to imagine life without modern entertainment, then contrasts today’s battery-powered toys with pioneer toys. This shows why comparison to the present organizes and introduces the historical topic.

The article names hopscotch, jump rope, hide-and-seek, and “Mother, May I?” as pioneer games still played today. This shows that readers can connect familiar games from their own lives to pioneer children.

The article centers on how pioneer children created entertainment despite having little money and few supplies. The sentence about making their own fun from available materials supports this idea because the examples develop it throughout.

Step 2 tells the maker to hold each end of the string so the button sits in the middle. This instruction concerns positioning because it specifies exactly where the button should be before it is swung.
Freezing water inside the can creates a hard support behind its thin metal wall. This support is helpful because it keeps the side of the can from being crushed inward while the holes are hammered.
Step 5 directs the maker to wrap the wire ends around the holes several times to “secure the handle.” That action shows that secure means to attach the handle firmly to the can.

The tin can lantern provided light inside dark cabins and barns while protecting the candle flame. Because lighting was useful for household work and safety, the lantern could benefit the entire family rather than only entertain children.
